prompt: Summarize recent research progress in reinforcement learning focused on enabling agents to explore efficiently and proactively under conditions of sparse rewards and constraints, respectively. Additionally, analyze and discuss the potential implications and insights this research provides for trajectory planning problems.
article:
Recent progress can be understood through one central shift: **exploration is no longer treated as generic stochastic dithering**. In sparse-reward RL, the most credible gains now come from **structured exploration**—directed goals, temporally meaningful novelty, hierarchical subgoals, search/memory, and world-model planning—rather than from plain action noise or undirected curiosity alone [[26]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2) [[31]](https://openreview.net/forum)(https://openreview.net/forum) [[32]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/b111aa30ab71255946b19b6bd4e68939-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/b111aa30ab71255946b19b6bd4e68939-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[33]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/6d7c4a0727e089ed6cdd3151cbe8d8ba-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/6d7c4a0727e089ed6cdd3151cbe8d8ba-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[28]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/516593a423838642a2eb4e9c5b9c7f44-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/516593a423838642a2eb4e9c5b9c7f44-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[29]](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.10044v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2602.10044v1) [[45]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.10995)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.10995). In constrained RL, the field has moved from treating safety as a post hoc penalty toward **proactive feasibility management** through constrained updates, safe-set expansion, barrier/shielding layers, uncertainty-aware world models, and risk/chance formulations that try to keep learning itself safe, not just the final policy [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2) [[64]](https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2410.09486v2)(https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2410.09486v2) [[81]](https://neurips.cc/virtual/2023/poster/71024)(https://neurips.cc/virtual/2023/poster/71024) [[92]](http://www.cds.caltech.edu/~murray/preprints/comb19-aiaa.pdf)(http://www.cds.caltech.edu/~murray/preprints/comb19-aiaa.pdf) [[198]](https://tugraz.elsevierpure.com/ws/portalfiles/portal/93576031/Shields_for_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning.pdf)(https://tugraz.elsevierpure.com/ws/portalfiles/portal/93576031/Shields_for_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning.pdf) [[195]](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v168/pfrommer22a/pfrommer22a.pdf)(https://proceedings.mlr.press/v168/pfrommer22a/pfrommer22a.pdf).

For **trajectory planning**, the main implication is not that RL replaces planners. The stronger pattern is that RL contributes best when it supplies **local skills, learned steering, uncertainty estimates, subgoal abstractions, or map/world priors**, while classical planners still dominate when explicit geometry, feasibility, compositional structure, or guarantees matter most [[252]](https://research.google/pubs/rl-rrt-kinodynamic-motion-planning-via-learning-reachability-estimators-from-rl-policies/)(https://research.google/pubs/rl-rrt-kinodynamic-motion-planning-via-learning-reachability-estimators-from-rl-policies/) [[253]](https://www.ifaamas.org/Proceedings/aamas2022/pdfs/p35.pdf)(https://www.ifaamas.org/Proceedings/aamas2022/pdfs/p35.pdf) [[145]](https://www.kavrakilab.org/publications/orthey2024-review-sampling.pdf)(https://www.kavrakilab.org/publications/orthey2024-review-sampling.pdf) [[266]](https://www.roboticsproceedings.org/rss20/p118.pdf)(https://www.roboticsproceedings.org/rss20/p118.pdf) [[269]](https://www.roboticsproceedings.org/rss19/p064.pdf)(https://www.roboticsproceedings.org/rss19/p064.pdf) [[272]](https://www.annualreviews.org/content/journals/10.1146/annurev-control-030323-022510)(https://www.annualreviews.org/content/journals/10.1146/annurev-control-030323-022510). That is why the most promising systems are increasingly **hybrid planner-learning pipelines** rather than pure end-to-end policies [[147]](https://moveit.picknik.ai/main/doc/examples/hybrid_planning/hybrid_planning_tutorial.html)(https://moveit.picknik.ai/main/doc/examples/hybrid_planning/hybrid_planning_tutorial.html) [[148]](https://arxiv.org/html/2512.24651v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2512.24651v1) [[149]](https://arxiv.org/html/2603.13888v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2603.13888v1) [[163]](https://rlj.cs.umass.edu/2025/papers/RLJ_RLC_2025_214.pdf)(https://rlj.cs.umass.edu/2025/papers/RLJ_RLC_2025_214.pdf) [[274]](https://www.roboticsproceedings.org/rss18/p033.pdf)(https://www.roboticsproceedings.org/rss18/p033.pdf).

A more precise statement of the evidence is this: the field has not found one universally dominant algorithmic family. Instead, it has identified a **division of labor**. Methods that increase **coverage** of reward-relevant or information-relevant states work best for sparse rewards; methods that preserve **feasibility** of visited states or actions work best for safety; and the strongest practical systems combine both by separating “where to go next” from “what actions are admissible right now.” That separation is what links recent RL progress to trajectory planning.

## Unified comparison of the main algorithmic families

The table below consolidates the decision-relevant comparison across the major families discussed in the report. It merges what would otherwise be scattered method-by-method judgments into one operational view.

| Family | Core mechanism | Representative methods already discussed | Strongest setting | What it improves most | What it usually does **not** solve by itself | Main planning translation | Recommended when... |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Intrinsic motivation / curiosity | Add internal reward for prediction error, uncertainty, or novelty | EXPLORS, ETD, ELDEN, EIPO | Sparse feedback before first extrinsic reward | Early exploration signal; medium-horizon discovery | Long-horizon structure, noisy-TV distraction, feasibility | Information bonus, uncertainty-guided rollout scoring | Reward is sparse but unsafe regions are not dominant and representation quality is decent |
| Count / novelty in learned embeddings | Estimate visitation counts or density in representation space | RECODE, E3B-style analyses | Low- to medium-dimensional structure, repeated state revisitation | Coverage tracking over many episodes | Continuous high-dimensional spaces with poor embeddings | Frontier scoring, graph node novelty | You can learn a planning-relevant embedding and revisit states often |
| Goal-conditioned + hindsight | Relabel achieved states as goals; choose exploratory subgoals | HER, DISCOVER | Long-horizon sparse goal-reaching | Credit assignment and directional exploration | Hard statewise safety without extra feasibility layer | Waypoint discovery, subgoal planners | The task has meaningful intermediate states and long horizons |
| Hierarchical / skill discovery | Learn temporally extended actions or latent subgoals | HIQL, PTGM, METRA | Very long horizon, reusable subroutines | Effective horizon reduction | Exact safety guarantees, recovery from unseen hazards | Abstract route planning plus skill execution | Local skills repeat across tasks or maps |
| Model-based / world models | Learn dynamics and plan in imagination | DreamSmooth, optimistic world models, safe world models | Data-limited or long-horizon problems | Sample efficiency, lookahead | Robust exploration if the model is certainty-equivalent or miscalibrated | MPC, imagined trajectory rollout, belief updates | You can afford model-learning effort and need foresight |
| Search-RL hybrids | Explicit memory/search over promising states | Go-Explore, graph correction, planner layers on offline data | Deep exploration with bottlenecks | Returning to frontier states and branching deeper | End-to-end simplicity; sometimes expensive state bookkeeping | Frontier caches, graph search, route reuse | Hard exploration comes from bottlenecks or rare action sequences |
| Lagrangian / primal-dual CMDP | Optimize reward subject to expected cost budgets | RCPO-style methods, adaptive primal-dual, constrained actor-critic | Expected-cost safety budgets | General constrained optimization with standard RL machinery | Strict no-violation safety during learning | Cost-budgeted planning or energy-aware control | Expected cumulative cost is the real requirement and some violations are tolerable during training |
| Trust-region constrained updates | Enforce approximate feasibility at each policy update | CPO, PCPO, CUP-like families | High-dimensional policy search with update instability | More stable constrained updates | Infeasibility when trust region and feasible set do not intersect | Conservative policy improvement in planner-learned controllers | Policy-update safety matters and second-order machinery is acceptable |
| Barrier / Lyapunov / shielding | Runtime correction or blocking of unsafe actions | CBF filters, learned barriers, shielding | Safety-critical online execution | Training-time survivability and action-level safety | Discovery of long-horizon sparse rewards | Safety filter around planner or learned skill | One unsafe action is unacceptable and a model/specification exists |
| Safe-set expansion / reachability | Expand verified region from known-safe seed | ACTSAFE, RCRL, RESPO, VELM | Persistent state constraints; safe exploration under uncertainty | Statewise feasibility guarantees over verified regions | Scalability without safe seed or calibrated uncertainty | Reachable-safe region planning | You have an initial safe controller or seed set and strong safety semantics |
| Chance-constrained / CVaR / DRO | Optimize tail risk or probability of failure | CVaR RL, chance-constrained and robust methods | Rare-catastrophe domains under uncertainty | Better alignment with crash probability / tail safety | Cheap optimization; may become conservative | Risk-aware belief-space and motion planning | Mean cost is the wrong safety object |
| Joint sparse+safe hybrids | Combine subgoals/planning/curricula with safety control | SaGui, SCG, CLiC, constrained goal-conditioned graph planning, CERL | Sparse long-horizon tasks with narrow feasible regions | Both coverage and feasibility when well designed | Universal transfer across tasks; usually architecture-heavy | Hybrid planner-learning stack | The task is simultaneously sparse, long-horizon, and safety-constrained |

**Best-for summary:** if the bottleneck is **discovering reward**, prefer goal-conditioned, hierarchical, search-based, or model-based structure; if the bottleneck is **staying safe while learning**, prefer shields, barriers, safe sets, or risk-aware formulations; if both hold, the most credible choice is a **hybrid** in which exploration proposes subgoals/routes and a separate safety layer filters or certifies execution.

## Problem setting, scope, and crucial distinctions

The core formal settings remain **MDPs** for fully observed control, **POMDPs/belief-space formulations** for partial observability, and **CMDPs** for explicit constraints [[1]](https://mediatum.ub.tum.de/doc/1696451/document.pdf)(https://mediatum.ub.tum.de/doc/1696451/document.pdf) [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2) [[4]](https://groups.csail.mit.edu/robotics-center/public_papers/Platt10.pdf)(https://groups.csail.mit.edu/robotics-center/public_papers/Platt10.pdf) [[5]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1744791/FULLTEXT01.pdf)(https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1744791/FULLTEXT01.pdf). For this review, the relevant recent window is approximately **2022–2026**, because that period contains the recent algorithmic families, benchmark standardization efforts, and newer theoretical guarantees referenced below [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2) [[26]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2) [[216]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf) [[222]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/ecd92623ac899357312aaa8915853699-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/ecd92623ac899357312aaa8915853699-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[237]](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.15076v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2602.15076v1).

Formally, the unconstrained control problem is typically an MDP with states, actions, transition law, reward, initial-state distribution, and discount factor, and the standard objective is the discounted return \(J(\pi)=\mathbb{E}_\pi[\sum_t \gamma^t r(s_t,a_t)]\) [[1]](https://mediatum.ub.tum.de/doc/1696451/document.pdf)(https://mediatum.ub.tum.de/doc/1696451/document.pdf) [[2]](https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/10197913/1/A_Review_of_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning_Methods_Theories_and_Applications.pdf)(https://www.researchgate.net/publication/360782013_A_Review_of_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning_Methods_Theory_and_Applications) [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2). For partial observability, robotics usually faces a POMDP or its belief-space equivalent because the controller acts on noisy, incomplete observations rather than the full state; exact solution is PSPACE-complete even for finite-horizon discrete settings, which is why approximations such as Gaussian belief-space planning and receding-horizon belief updates remain common [[4]](https://groups.csail.mit.edu/robotics-center/public_papers/Platt10.pdf)(https://groups.csail.mit.edu/robotics-center/public_papers/Platt10.pdf) [[5]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1744791/FULLTEXT01.pdf)(https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1744791/FULLTEXT01.pdf). For safety or resource limits, the dominant abstraction is the CMDP, in which one or more cost functions \(c^{(i)}(s,a)\) encode unsafe behavior or budget usage and the policy must maximize reward subject to \(J_{c^{(i)}}(\pi)\le d_i\) for all constraints [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2) [[2]](https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/10197913/1/A_Review_of_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning_Methods_Theories_and_Applications.pdf)(https://www.researchgate.net/publication/360782013_A_Review_of_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning_Methods_Theory_and_Applications) [[6]](https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf)(https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf).

That formal distinction matters because it changes what can be guaranteed. In a finite CMDP, an optimal stationary policy exists, but with multiple simultaneous constraints it may need to be stochastic rather than deterministic to satisfy all bounds exactly [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2). It also matters because **safe RL and robust RL are not identical**: robust RL addresses model uncertainty or adversarial disturbance, whereas safe RL addresses explicit feasibility or risk requirements, although the two are often combined in practice [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2).

Two distinctions matter immediately.

### Sparse rewards are not the same as hard exploration

A **sparse reward** task is one in which informative extrinsic reward appears rarely, so random behavior is unlikely to observe successful trajectories often enough for efficient learning [[19]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11340-0)(https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11340-0) [[134]](https://www.oaepublish.com/articles/ir.2023.10)(https://www.oaepublish.com/articles/ir.2023.10). A **hard-exploration** task is broader: it is the resulting search problem when the agent must discover rare successful action sequences over long horizons, often under deceptive rewards, narrow feasible regions, large state spaces, or strong path dependence [[19]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11340-0)(https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11340-0) [[176]](https://arxiv.org/html/2603.05842v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2603.05842v1) [[178]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-25949-7)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-25949-7). In trajectory planning, hard exploration can arise even with some dense shaping, because the true feasible set may still be very narrow due to obstacles, dynamics, or interaction constraints [[176]](https://arxiv.org/html/2603.05842v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2603.05842v1) [[178]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-25949-7)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-25949-7).

This is not a semantic nicety; it changes which algorithmic interventions are sensible. Sparse feedback means the policy lacks gradient signal. Hard exploration means the policy lacks **access** to the successful part of the state-action graph. If the real issue is only sparse feedback, then hindsight relabeling or auxiliary rewards may be enough [[26]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2) [[134]](https://www.oaepublish.com/articles/ir.2023.10)(https://www.oaepublish.com/articles/ir.2023.10). If the issue is a **narrow feasible corridor** or coupled interaction geometry, then the agent often needs **subgoal structure, planning memory, curriculum, resets, or feasibility priors**, not just more curiosity [[176]](https://arxiv.org/html/2603.05842v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2603.05842v1) [[178]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-25949-7)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-25949-7) [[179]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.10658)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.10658).

A useful planning analogy is the difference between “the map gives little reward feedback” and “the only successful route is a one-meter-wide passage behind three irreversible turns.” The former is mostly a signal problem; the latter is a search-geometry problem.

### Constraints are not all the same thing

Recent safe-RL work makes a sharp distinction between **expected cumulative cost constraints**, **state constraints**, **joint chance constraints**, **instantaneous constraints**, and **risk-sensitive criteria** such as **CVaR** [[115]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf)(https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf) [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2) [[182]](https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~shangding.gu/tutorials/Safe_RL_2024_SmartGridComm.pdf)(https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~shangding.gu/tutorials/Safe_RL_2024_SmartGridComm.pdf). A CMDP cost can encode **unsafe behavior** or **resource usage**, so the same mathematics can represent safety budgets, energy budgets, damage, or communication load [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2). But risk-sensitive criteria such as CVaR are not just another additive cost: they alter the optimization structure and often break the linear machinery that makes classic CMDPs convenient [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2).

The IJCAI 2024 survey makes this concrete by distinguishing expected cumulative-cost constraints, state constraints based on entering unsafe sets, joint chance constraints on ever visiting unsafe states, expected instantaneous constraints with time-varying thresholds, and almost-sure cumulative constraints [[115]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf)(https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf). Those formulations are not interchangeable. They induce different feasible policy sets and, in practice, different levels of conservatism. A conservative additive approximation to a joint chance constraint can preserve safety but also shrink the exploratory frontier so aggressively that the agent never reaches the sparse goal region [[115]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf)(https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf).

For planning, the semantic difference is decisive. If the real requirement is “**never collide**,” then an average discounted collision cost is often too weak [[115]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf)(https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf) [[275]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/10a3b1c30b8cceb507b9e8ddcc9a1a6a-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/10a3b1c30b8cceb507b9e8ddcc9a1a6a-Paper-Conference.pdf). If the requirement is “**keep failure probability below δ**,” a chance-constrained or risk-sensitive formulation is more faithful [[115]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf)(https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf) [[195]](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v168/pfrommer22a/pfrommer22a.pdf)(https://proceedings.mlr.press/v168/pfrommer22a/pfrommer22a.pdf) [[265]](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v205/nishimura23a/nishimura23a.pdf)(https://proceedings.mlr.press/v205/nishimura23a/nishimura23a.pdf). If the requirement is “**stay within actuator/torque/pressure limits at every step**,” instantaneous or barrier-style constraints are the more natural abstraction [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2) [[143]](https://www.dynsyslab.org/wp-content/papercite-data/pdf/dsl-annurev22.pdf)(https://www.dynsyslab.org/wp-content/papercite-data/pdf/dsl-annurev22.pdf) [[158]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12555-024-0784-5)(https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12555-024-0784-5).

### What should be measured

The literature does not yet offer one universal metric protocol, but a useful unified map is now visible.

For **sparse-reward exploration**, the most informative metrics are:
- **sample efficiency** or required interactions to reach target performance [[219]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/ad2b3b0963114adddbd2c97d6e74abb9-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/ad2b3b0963114adddbd2c97d6e74abb9-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[223]](https://icaps24.icaps-conference.org/program/workshops/prl-papers/8.pdf)(https://icaps24.icaps-conference.org/program/workshops/prl-papers/8.pdf)
- **success rate** on downstream tasks or goal-reaching [[219]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/ad2b3b0963114adddbd2c97d6e74abb9-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/ad2b3b0963114adddbd2c97d6e74abb9-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[222]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/ecd92623ac899357312aaa8915853699-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/ecd92623ac899357312aaa8915853699-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[224]](https://arxiv.org/html/2306.06192v8)(https://arxiv.org/html/2306.06192v8)
- **time to first/second/third reward discovery** in truly sparse domains [[19]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11340-0)(https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11340-0) [[215]](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.11533v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2501.11533v1)
- **coverage** measures such as observation/state/position coverage or visitation diversity [[19]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11340-0)(https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00521-025-11340-0) [[215]](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.11533v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2501.11533v1)

For **safe/constrained learning**, the key metrics are:
- **final reward / task performance** [[6]](https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf)(https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf) [[216]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf) [[218]](https://www.offline-saferl.org/)(https://www.offline-saferl.org/)
- **final cost / constraint satisfaction** relative to budget [[6]](https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf)(https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf) [[216]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf) [[218]](https://www.offline-saferl.org/)(https://www.offline-saferl.org/)
- **training-time safety regret or violation behavior** during learning, not just at convergence [[6]](https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf)(https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf) [[216]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf)
- **normalized cost return**, often as cost divided by threshold, where values below 1 mean budget satisfaction [[218]](https://www.offline-saferl.org/)(https://www.offline-saferl.org/)

For **trajectory planning**, these need to be augmented with domain metrics:
- **collision rate / safe rate / minimum clearance** [[177]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9108367/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9108367/) [[183]](https://openreview.net/pdf/85a54a2b3c26f13e2ad7b2043422ba70a4b807c8.pdf)(https://openreview.net/pdf/85a54a2b3c26f13e2ad7b2043422ba70a4b807c8.pdf)
- **path length / path efficiency / runtime / lap time / average speed / energy** [[176]](https://arxiv.org/html/2603.05842v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2603.05842v1) [[178]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-25949-7)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-25949-7) [[183]](https://openreview.net/pdf/85a54a2b3c26f13e2ad7b2043422ba70a4b807c8.pdf)(https://openreview.net/pdf/85a54a2b3c26f13e2ad7b2043422ba70a4b807c8.pdf) [[224]](https://arxiv.org/html/2306.06192v8)(https://arxiv.org/html/2306.06192v8)
- **smoothness / jerk / comfort / continuity of velocities and joint angles** [[178]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-25949-7)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-25949-7) [[184]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2306.15136)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2306.15136) [[161]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-93175-2)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-93175-2)
- **uncertainty-aware metrics** such as belief uncertainty, risk score, or calibration quality where appropriate [[5]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1744791/FULLTEXT01.pdf)(https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1744791/FULLTEXT01.pdf) [[183]](https://openreview.net/pdf/85a54a2b3c26f13e2ad7b2043422ba70a4b807c8.pdf)(https://openreview.net/pdf/85a54a2b3c26f13e2ad7b2043422ba70a4b807c8.pdf) [[280]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/8451a20c5a7e0ee5671dda28f7daf7f3-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/8451a20c5a7e0ee5671dda28f7daf7f3-Paper-Conference.pdf)

The major methodological lesson is that **reward alone is an inadequate evaluation metric** for both sparse exploration and safe planning, because high return can coexist with poor motion quality, unsafe tails, or heavy reliance on reward shaping artifacts [[10]](https://etheses.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/38454/1/Multifaceted%20Performance%20Metrics%20for%20RL.pdf)(https://etheses.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/38454/1/Multifaceted%20Performance%20Metrics%20for%20RL.pdf) [[184]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2306.15136)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2306.15136) [[225]](https://rlj.cs.umass.edu/2025/papers/RLJ_RLC_2025_140.pdf)(https://rlj.cs.umass.edu/2025/papers/RLJ_RLC_2025_140.pdf) [[226]](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.10550v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2502.10550v2). A planner that reaches the goal 95% of the time but clips safety margins, uses excessive jerk, or violates cost budgets is not equivalent to one that reaches the goal with larger clearance and lower risk. The literature increasingly reflects that distinction, but many papers still report return first and safety/trajectory quality second.

## Recent progress in efficient exploration under sparse rewards

The main empirical pattern across 2022–2026 is that **undirected exploration is no longer enough for long-horizon sparse tasks**. The strongest results come from methods that inject **structure** into what counts as novelty, how goals are chosen, how long-horizon behavior is decomposed, or how search/planning is layered on top of learning [[26]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2) [[31]](https://openreview.net/forum)(https://openreview.net/forum) [[32]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/b111aa30ab71255946b19b6bd4e68939-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/b111aa30ab71255946b19b6bd4e68939-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[33]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/6d7c4a0727e089ed6cdd3151cbe8d8ba-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/6d7c4a0727e089ed6cdd3151cbe8d8ba-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[28]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/516593a423838642a2eb4e9c5b9c7f44-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/516593a423838642a2eb4e9c5b9c7f44-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[34]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/hash/31ceb5aed43e2ec1b132e389cc1dcb56-Abstract-Conference.html)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/hash/31ceb5aed43e2ec1b132e389cc1dcb56-Abstract-Conference.html) [[45]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.10995)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.10995).

The underlying causal reason is consistent across papers: if success depends on a long sequence of correlated decisions, then local randomness has exponentially small probability of discovering the useful trajectory. Recent methods therefore change the **distribution of attempted trajectories**, not just their variance. That is the substantive shift.

### 1. Intrinsic motivation is still important, but naïve curiosity is no longer the leading story

Prediction-error curiosity and related intrinsic rewards remain a major family because they create learning signal before extrinsic reward is observed [[54]](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.19725v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2507.19725v1) [[35]](https://arxiv.org/html/2302.11563v4)(https://arxiv.org/html/2302.11563v4). But multiple recent papers emphasize that plain prediction error is fragile in stochastic environments and can chase inherently noisy or irrelevant dynamics, the classic **“noisy TV”** pathology [[29]](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.10044v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2602.10044v1) [[32]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/b111aa30ab71255946b19b6bd4e68939-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/b111aa30ab71255946b19b6bd4e68939-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[35]](https://arxiv.org/html/2302.11563v4)(https://arxiv.org/html/2302.11563v4).

Recent work improves this in two ways.

First, methods change **what uncertainty is measured**. **ETD** replaces ad hoc novelty with **temporal distance**, arguing that successor-distance-style novelty is more robust and representation-invariant than raw count bonuses or Euclidean latent distances [[32]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/b111aa30ab71255946b19b6bd4e68939-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/b111aa30ab71255946b19b6bd4e68939-Paper-Conference.pdf). The key causal claim is not merely “better novelty,” but that temporal distance discounts superficial sensory variation and instead highlights states that are meaningfully far away in controllable dynamics. In planning language, it is closer to a learned geodesic over reachable behavior than to a pixel-space distance. **ELDEN** targets uncertainty over **entity dependencies** rather than exact next-state prediction, which is especially helpful when sparse-reward tasks depend on discovering causal interactions among objects rather than merely visiting visually novel states [[30]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/31ed129feae64a7e44a15b148c15558d-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/31ed129feae64a7e44a15b148c15558d-Paper-Conference.pdf). That matters in manipulation or puzzle-like domains where the decisive discovery is often an interaction graph, not a location.

Second, methods regulate **when intrinsic reward should matter**. **EIPO** argues that intrinsic rewards help only while exploration is genuinely needed; once extrinsic reward becomes learnable, always-on intrinsic reward can bias the objective and harm performance [[211]](https://williamd4112.github.io/pubs/neurips22_eipo.pdf)(https://williamd4112.github.io/pubs/neurips22_eipo.pdf). Its constrained-optimization framing adaptively suppresses intrinsic reward when further exploration is unnecessary, and the paper reports consistent gains across **61 Atari games** with explicit evidence that exploration weight falls early and stabilizes once extrinsic reward becomes reachable [[211]](https://williamd4112.github.io/pubs/neurips22_eipo.pdf)(https://williamd4112.github.io/pubs/neurips22_eipo.pdf). That is important because many earlier curiosity methods silently mixed two incompatible objectives: “discover novel states” and “maximize task reward.” EIPO’s contribution is to explicitly control the tradeoff instead of assuming the same exploration pressure is optimal throughout training.

**EXPLORS** adds a related lesson: exploration bonus alone and self-supervised reward shaping alone each fail in different ways, so combining a learned intrinsic reward \(R_\phi\) with a separate exploration bonus \(B_w\) can be more robust in environments with “extremely sparse or distracting” reward [[98]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2022/hash/266c0f191b04cbbbe529016d0edc847e-Abstract-Conference.html)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2022/file/ad2b3b0963114adddbd2c97d6e74abb9-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[99]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2022/file/266c0f191b04cbbbe529016d0edc847e-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2022/file/ad2b3b0963114adddbd2c97d6e74abb9-Paper-Conference.pdf). The causal point is that exploration and reward shaping solve different subproblems: one increases state coverage, the other converts sparse external signal into denser learning gradients once the signal has been encountered at least occasionally.

The practical takeaway is that modern intrinsic motivation works best when it is **task-timed and representation-aware**, not simply added on top of extrinsic reward forever [[211]](https://williamd4112.github.io/pubs/neurips22_eipo.pdf)(https://williamd4112.github.io/pubs/neurips22_eipo.pdf) [[32]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/b111aa30ab71255946b19b6bd4e68939-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/b111aa30ab71255946b19b6bd4e68939-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[30]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/31ed129feae64a7e44a15b148c15558d-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/31ed129feae64a7e44a15b148c15558d-Paper-Conference.pdf).

### 2. Count- and novelty-based exploration remain competitive if the representation is right

Recent work does not reject count-based exploration; it reframes it as a **representation problem**. **RECODE** explicitly argues that count bonuses in large or continuous state spaces only work if novelty is counted in an embedding that preserves meaningful long-term distinctions, and it introduces an online clustering-based novelty estimator in learned representation space [[31]](https://openreview.net/forum)(https://openreview.net/forum). The paper’s practical claim that counts can be tracked over **thousands of episodes** matters because it addresses a real weakness of episodic-only novelty signals: they reset too often to support long-horizon discovery.

This is why recent count-style methods spend as much effort on representation learning as on the count bonus itself [[31]](https://openreview.net/forum)(https://openreview.net/forum) [[32]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/b111aa30ab71255946b19b6bd4e68939-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/b111aa30ab71255946b19b6bd4e68939-Paper-Conference.pdf). The broader empirical pattern is that count-like ideas remain strong in low-dimensional or well-structured spaces, but degrade under raw pixels or rapidly varying contexts unless supported by better embeddings [[31]](https://openreview.net/forum)(https://openreview.net/forum) [[32]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/b111aa30ab71255946b19b6bd4e68939-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/b111aa30ab71255946b19b6bd4e68939-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[36]](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v202/henaff23a/henaff23a.pdf)(https://proceedings.mlr.press/v202/henaff23a/henaff23a.pdf) [[215]](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.11533v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2501.11533v1). That finding is consistent with empirical comparisons showing that simple state counts can outperform more elaborate intrinsic objectives in low-dimensional domains, while entropy-based or representation-robust methods hold up better under RGB observations [[215]](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.11533v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2501.11533v1).

The operative mechanism is easy to miss: count bonuses are only useful when two semantically similar states map to nearby latent codes and when revisitation of a bottleneck is recognized as “not novel anymore.” If every frame is effectively unique, the count bonus becomes nearly uniform and stops guiding exploration.

### 3. Goal-conditioned and hindsight-based exploration have become central

A major shift in sparse-reward RL is the move from pure novelty to **goal-conditioned exploration**. The idea is simple: even if the intended sparse goal is rarely achieved, trajectories typically achieve *some* states, and those achieved states can be relabeled as goals for learning via **HER**-style hindsight updates [[26]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2) [[58]](https://arxiv.org/html/2603.21357v3)(https://arxiv.org/html/2603.21357v3) [[134]](https://www.oaepublish.com/articles/ir.2023.10)(https://www.oaepublish.com/articles/ir.2023.10).

Recent progress pushes that idea further by making exploratory goals **directed rather than arbitrary**. **DISCOVER** argues that many prior methods explore in ways suited to solving many tasks, whereas a single sparse high-dimensional target is better approached through **intermediate goals that are simultaneously achievable, relevant to the target, and novel** [[26]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2). Its claimed theoretical bound depends on shortest start-to-target distance rather than the volume of the whole goal space, which is exactly the kind of structural dependence that matters in long-horizon planning [[26]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2).

This is one of the clearest places where causal structure improved. Undirected curiosity asks, “What have I not seen?” Directed goal exploration asks, “What achievable state would most reduce the remaining distance to the task?” Those are not equivalent. In long-horizon control, the second question is usually the right one.

The planning relevance is immediate: this is essentially a learned analogue of **subgoal decomposition** and **waypoint planning**, except the waypoints are selected adaptively from value and uncertainty structure rather than from geometry alone [[26]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2) [[212]](https://neurips.cc/virtual/2024/101066)(https://neurips.cc/virtual/2024/101066).

### 4. Hierarchical and skill-based methods matter because they shorten the effective horizon

For long-horizon sparse tasks, the key difficulty is not only discovering reward but also assigning credit across many steps. Hierarchical methods address this by learning **subgoals, latent options, or skills** that transform a single long-horizon task into shorter local problems [[39]](https://www.mdpi.com/2504-4990/4/1/9)(https://www.mdpi.com/2504-4990/4/1/9) [[33]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/6d7c4a0727e089ed6cdd3151cbe8d8ba-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/6d7c4a0727e089ed6cdd3151cbe8d8ba-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[40]](https://github.com/PKU-RL/PTGM)(https://github.com/PKU-RL/PTGM).

**HIQL** is especially important because it shows that hierarchy can improve long-horizon goal-reaching even in offline settings where value estimates for faraway goals are noisy [[33]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/6d7c4a0727e089ed6cdd3151cbe8d8ba-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/6d7c4a0727e089ed6cdd3151cbe8d8ba-Paper-Conference.pdf). Its mechanism is to learn a high-level policy over latent subgoals and a low-level controller that reaches them, improving the **signal-to-noise ratio** of value-based extraction for distant goals [[33]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/6d7c4a0727e089ed6cdd3151cbe8d8ba-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/6d7c4a0727e089ed6cdd3151cbe8d8ba-Paper-Conference.pdf). That is exactly the same structural reason hierarchical planning helps in robotics: long-range planning is easier when one reasons over **abstract intermediate states** rather than raw controls [[33]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/6d7c4a0727e089ed6cdd3151cbe8d8ba-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/6d7c4a0727e089ed6cdd3151cbe8d8ba-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[130]](https://pure.uva.nl/ws/files/72141008/Hierarchies_of_Planning_and_Reinforcement_Learning_for_Robot_Navigation.pdf)(https://pure.uva.nl/ws/files/72141008/Hierarchies_of_Planning_and_Reinforcement_Learning_for_Robot_Navigation.pdf).

Other recent work such as **PTGM** and **METRA** pushes hierarchy through pretraining and temporally meaningful latent spaces, again reducing the effective planning horizon rather than solving the sparse task end to end from scratch [[40]](https://github.com/PKU-RL/PTGM)(https://github.com/PKU-RL/PTGM) [[28]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/516593a423838642a2eb4e9c5b9c7f44-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/516593a423838642a2eb4e9c5b9c7f44-Paper-Conference.pdf). The causal pattern is the same: if the high-level controller only needs to decide every \(k\) steps, and the low-level skill can reliably realize each subgoal, then both exploration and credit assignment become materially easier.

### 5. World models are resurging, but exploration quality inside the model is the real bottleneck

Model-based RL promises better sample efficiency because learned dynamics support imagined rollouts and planning [[29]](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.10044v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2602.10044v1) [[44]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/6f7d90b1198fec96defd80b5ebd5bc81-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/6f7d90b1198fec96defd80b5ebd5bc81-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[273]](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.16680v3)(https://arxiv.org/html/2504.16680v3). But recent sparse-reward papers make clear that learned world models are not automatically good explorers.

Two diagnoses recur. **DreamSmooth** argues that sparse reward itself is hard for world models because exact reward timing is ambiguous, making reward prediction a bottleneck [[44]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/6f7d90b1198fec96defd80b5ebd5bc81-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/6f7d90b1198fec96defd80b5ebd5bc81-Paper-Conference.pdf). **Optimistic World Models** instead argues that standard world-model training is too **certainty-equivalent** and insufficiently optimistic for sparse tasks, so imagined rollouts underexplore unless the model-learning objective is biased toward better-rewarding outcomes [[29]](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.10044v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2602.10044v1).

The important synthesis is that world models help sparse-reward exploration only when they do more than predict dynamics. They need mechanisms for **optimism, uncertainty handling, or planning correction**; otherwise they simply reproduce the exploration failures of model-free agents in imagination [[29]](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.10044v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2602.10044v1) [[44]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/6f7d90b1198fec96defd80b5ebd5bc81-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/6f7d90b1198fec96defd80b5ebd5bc81-Paper-Conference.pdf).

This is also where calibration becomes decisive. A world model that is inaccurate but overconfident does not just reduce sample efficiency; it can push the planner toward phantom corridors or fictitious low-risk routes. In robotics, that failure mode matters more than modest reward loss.

### 6. Search-RL hybrids remain the most convincing answer for truly deep exploration

The strongest hard-exploration successes still come from methods that preserve explicit **memory, return-to-frontier behavior, or search structure** rather than relying purely on local stochastic exploration [[45]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.10995)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.10995) [[46]](https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.10995)(https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.10995) [[38]](https://neurips.cc/virtual/2023/poster/71695)(https://neurips.cc/virtual/2023/poster/71695). **Go-Explore** remains the canonical example: remember promising states, return to them deterministically, then explore further from there [[45]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.10995)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.10995) [[46]](https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.10995)(https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.10995). Recent work continues this philosophy through graph-based correction of value landscapes, frontier-based subgoal search, and planner layers over offline exploration data [[34]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/hash/31ceb5aed43e2ec1b132e389cc1dcb56-Abstract-Conference.html)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/hash/31ceb5aed43e2ec1b132e389cc1dcb56-Abstract-Conference.html) [[38]](https://neurips.cc/virtual/2023/poster/71695)(https://neurips.cc/virtual/2023/poster/71695) [[31]](https://openreview.net/forum)(https://openreview.net/forum).

This family is highly relevant to trajectory planning because it mirrors what classical planners have long done: maintain explicit frontier structure, avoid forgetting reachable bottlenecks, and separate **reaching a promising state** from **branching further** [[45]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.10995)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.10995) [[251]](https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/eabe/b5a6bd995828898273763e49cf04fbaddb89.pdf)(https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/eabe/b5a6bd995828898273763e49cf04fbaddb89.pdf) [[260]](https://www2.eecs.berkeley.edu/Pubs/TechRpts/2025/EECS-2025-172.pdf)(https://www2.eecs.berkeley.edu/Pubs/TechRpts/2025/EECS-2025-172.pdf).

The broader conclusion across these families is not that curiosity failed. It is that curiosity became one component inside more structured exploration systems. The winners increasingly combine novelty with memory, goals, or planning.

## Proactive exploration under constraints: recent progress and trade-offs

The constrained side of the literature is more heterogeneous because it mixes different safety semantics and guarantee types. The most useful way to organize it is by **where safety is enforced**: in the objective, in the policy update, in the action filter, in a reachable safe set, or in a risk/uncertainty model [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2) [[189]](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v70/achiam17a/achiam17a.pdf)(https://proceedings.mlr.press/v70/achiam17a/achiam17a.pdf) [[198]](https://tugraz.elsevierpure.com/ws/portalfiles/portal/93576031/Shields_for_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning.pdf)(https://tugraz.elsevierpure.com/ws/portalfiles/portal/93576031/Shields_for_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning.pdf) [[64]](https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2410.09486v2)(https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2410.09486v2) [[195]](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v168/pfrommer22a/pfrommer22a.pdf)(https://proceedings.mlr.press/v168/pfrommer22a/pfrommer22a.pdf).

A central empirical tension cuts across all these families: many methods can produce a safe or nearly safe final policy, but far fewer can guarantee that the **data-collection process itself** remains safe. The ACTSAFE and VELM lines make this critique explicit [[64]](https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2410.09486v2)(https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2410.09486v2) [[65]](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-65633-0_11)(https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-65633-0_11). That is the main reason safe exploration remains harder than constrained optimization on paper.

## 1. CMDP/Lagrangian and primal-dual methods: still the default, but no longer the whole story

The default constrained formulation is still to maximize reward subject to expected cumulative cost budgets in a CMDP, solved through **Lagrangian** or **primal-dual** updates [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2) [[115]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf)(https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf). This remains attractive because it reuses standard policy-gradient and actor-critic machinery [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2). The standard Lagrangian \(\mathcal{L}(\pi,\lambda)=J(\pi)+\sum_i \lambda_i(d_i-J_{c^{(i)}}(\pi))\) yields a penalized reward form for fixed multipliers, which is exactly why this family scales so readily to deep RL codebases [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2).

Recent theory significantly strengthens this family. There are now results for:
- **nonstationary CMDPs** via primal-dual methods with dynamic regret and violation bounds [[69]](https://lavaei.ieor.berkeley.edu/Safe_RL_2022_1.pdf)(https://lavaei.ieor.berkeley.edu/Safe_RL_2022_1.pdf)
- **zero-violation conservative primal-dual** algorithms in discounted tabular settings with improved sample complexity [[70]](https://cdn.aaai.org/ojs/20281/20281-13-24294-1-2-20220628.pdf)(https://cdn.aaai.org/ojs/20281/20281-13-24294-1-2-20220628.pdf)
- **global convergence of natural policy gradient primal-dual schemes** with \(O(1/\sqrt{T})\) optimality-gap and violation rates and \(O(1/\epsilon^4)\) model-free sample complexity [[244]](https://www.jmlr.org/papers/volume26/22-0622/22-0622.pdf)(https://www.jmlr.org/papers/volume26/22-0622/22-0622.pdf)
- finite-time mirror-descent-type convergence for objective and constraint violation at \(O(1/\sqrt{T})\) [[245]](http://www.columbia.edu/~jd2736/publication/CMDP.pdf)(http://www.columbia.edu/~jd2736/publication/CMDP.pdf)

However, the strongest practical critique remains intact: **dual updates are only as good as cost estimation**, and off-policy methods in particular can badly underestimate cost, causing the multiplier to move in the wrong direction and thereby destabilize safety enforcement [[192]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/2f8b56543953d60f262fb2c4b85c50b3-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/2f8b56543953d60f262fb2c4b85c50b3-Paper-Conference.pdf). This is why recent off-policy safe RL adds **cost upper confidence bounds**, conservative policy optimization, or adaptive learning-rate control around the basic primal-dual loop [[192]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/2f8b56543953d60f262fb2c4b85c50b3-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/2f8b56543953d60f262fb2c4b85c50b3-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[191]](https://research.ibm.com/publications/adaptive-primal-dual-method-for-safe-reinforcement-learning)(https://research.ibm.com/publications/adaptive-primal-dual-method-for-safe-reinforcement-learning).

### What this family is good for
- generic CMDP formulations [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2) [[115]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf)(https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf)
- expected-cost budgets where exact statewise safety is not required [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2) [[115]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf)(https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf)
- reusing standard deep RL infrastructure [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)

### Main limitations
- can satisfy constraints only **asymptotically or in expectation**, not necessarily during learning [[64]](https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2410.09486v2)(https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2410.09486v2) [[14]](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v139/wagener21a/wagener21a.pdf)(https://proceedings.mlr.press/v139/wagener21a/wagener21a.pdf)
- sensitive to cost-estimation bias and dual-learning dynamics [[192]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/2f8b56543953d60f262fb2c4b85c50b3-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/2f8b56543953d60f262fb2c4b85c50b3-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[191]](https://research.ibm.com/publications/adaptive-primal-dual-method-for-safe-reinforcement-learning)(https://research.ibm.com/publications/adaptive-primal-dual-method-for-safe-reinforcement-learning)
- can oscillate around constraint boundaries [[216]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf) [[193]](https://safe-policy-optimization.readthedocs.io/)(https://safe-policy-optimization.readthedocs.io/)

The causal trade-off is clear: Lagrangian methods are flexible because they convert constraints into prices, but that same conversion weakens hard guarantees. If the price updates are delayed or cost critics are biased low, the agent explores too aggressively before the multipliers catch up.

## 2. Constrained policy optimization and trust-region families: stronger update-level safety, higher brittleness

**CPO** and related trust-region constrained update methods aim to keep each policy update approximately feasible, rather than relying purely on asymptotic multiplier correction [[189]](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v70/achiam17a/achiam17a.pdf)(https://proceedings.mlr.press/v70/achiam17a/achiam17a.pdf) [[182]](https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~shangding.gu/tutorials/Safe_RL_2024_SmartGridComm.pdf)(https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~shangding.gu/tutorials/Safe_RL_2024_SmartGridComm.pdf). This makes them conceptually appealing for proactive exploration because safety is supposed to be preserved throughout training.

The trade-off is computational and geometric. These methods depend on local surrogate approximations and trust-region intersection geometry, and can become infeasible when the reward-improving local region and the constraint-feasible region do not intersect [[182]](https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~shangding.gu/tutorials/Safe_RL_2024_SmartGridComm.pdf)(https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~shangding.gu/tutorials/Safe_RL_2024_SmartGridComm.pdf). That is not a rare corner case; it occurs exactly when the current policy is near a hard safety boundary or when the cost surrogate is steep and noisy. Benchmark evidence does not show uniform dominance: CPO-style methods often appear more stable than plain Lagrangian baselines, but still incur violations and can be less competitive than tuned Lagrangian or PID-Lagrangian variants on some benchmarks [[193]](https://safe-policy-optimization.readthedocs.io/)(https://safe-policy-optimization.readthedocs.io/) [[216]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf).

Projection-style first-order variants such as **PCPO**, **CUP**, and newer PPO-like constrained update methods aim to keep some of the safety of constrained updates while reducing second-order complexity [[31]](https://openreview.net/forum)(https://openreview.net/forum) [[190]](http://staff.ustc.edu.cn/~zkan/Papers/Bood%20Chapter/2024_Safe%20Optimization.pdf)(http://staff.ustc.edu.cn/~zkan/Papers/Bood%20Chapter/2024_Safe%20Optimization.pdf) [[194]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2025/0592.pdf)(https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2025/0592.pdf). The credible pattern is that these methods help when update instability is the main problem, but they do not remove the underlying tension between reward improvement and local feasibility [[194]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2025/0592.pdf)(https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2025/0592.pdf) [[193]](https://safe-policy-optimization.readthedocs.io/)(https://safe-policy-optimization.readthedocs.io/).

## 3. Lyapunov, barrier, and shielding approaches: stronger online safety, more modeling assumptions

A different family enforces safety through explicit certificates or action correction rather than through cost optimization alone.

### Lyapunov methods
Lyapunov-based safe RL transforms global cumulative safety conditions into local policy constraints using a feasible baseline policy and a Lyapunov function [[203]](http://papers.neurips.cc/paper/8032-a-lyapunov-based-approach-to-safe-reinforcement-learning.pdf)(http://papers.neurips.cc/paper/8032-a-lyapunov-based-approach-to-safe-reinforcement-learning.pdf). This can guarantee feasible policies during learning under the method’s assumptions, but tends to be conservative and depends on constructing suitable Lyapunov functions [[203]](http://papers.neurips.cc/paper/8032-a-lyapunov-based-approach-to-safe-reinforcement-learning.pdf)(http://papers.neurips.cc/paper/8032-a-lyapunov-based-approach-to-safe-reinforcement-learning.pdf). The conservatism is structural: the method must certify decrease conditions that are valid under uncertainty, so it often rejects actions that are probably safe but not provably so.

### Control barrier functions
Barrier methods maintain forward invariance of a safe set, often through a quadratic-program filter that minimally corrects the nominal action [[73]](https://calinbelta.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Safe-exploration-in-model-based.pdf)(https://calinbelta.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Safe-exploration-in-model-based.pdf) [[92]](http://www.cds.caltech.edu/~murray/preprints/comb19-aiaa.pdf)(http://www.cds.caltech.edu/~murray/preprints/comb19-aiaa.pdf) [[75]](http://staff.ustc.edu.cn/~zkan/Papers/Conference/[42]_2022CAC.pdf)(http://staff.ustc.edu.cn/~zkan/Papers/Conference/ [[42]](https://realworld-sdm.github.io/paper/44.pdf)_2022CAC.pdf). The key benefit is that safety becomes an **execution-time property**, not just an expected training outcome [[92]](http://www.cds.caltech.edu/~murray/preprints/comb19-aiaa.pdf)(http://www.cds.caltech.edu/~murray/preprints/comb19-aiaa.pdf) [[73]](https://calinbelta.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Safe-exploration-in-model-based.pdf)(https://calinbelta.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Safe-exploration-in-model-based.pdf). Recent work extends this through learned barrier functions and online updates from data, including real robot experiments with quadrotors [[75]](http://staff.ustc.edu.cn/~zkan/Papers/Conference/[42]_2022CAC.pdf)(http://staff.ustc.edu.cn/~zkan/Papers/Conference/ [[42]](https://realworld-sdm.github.io/paper/44.pdf)_2022CAC.pdf). For planning, that means one can let an RL or search module propose aggressive maneuvers while reserving a certified last-mile correction layer.

### Shielding
Shielding blocks or overrides unsafe actions at runtime based on a model and a safety specification [[198]](https://tugraz.elsevierpure.com/ws/portalfiles/portal/93576031/Shields_for_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning.pdf)(https://tugraz.elsevierpure.com/ws/portalfiles/portal/93576031/Shields_for_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning.pdf). In theory, this can provide very strong guarantees—absolute or probabilistic depending on the model class [[198]](https://tugraz.elsevierpure.com/ws/portalfiles/portal/93576031/Shields_for_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning.pdf)(https://tugraz.elsevierpure.com/ws/portalfiles/portal/93576031/Shields_for_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning.pdf). In practice, shielding is highly effective for **preventing unsafe data collection**, but it often **stabilizes learning more than it improves deep exploration itself** [[208]](https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/26723/26495)(https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/26723/26495) [[198]](https://tugraz.elsevierpure.com/ws/portalfiles/portal/93576031/Shields_for_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning.pdf)(https://tugraz.elsevierpure.com/ws/portalfiles/portal/93576031/Shields_for_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning.pdf). The AAAI 2023 partial-observability study says this explicitly: shielding can improve convergence by avoiding crash-heavy episodes and extending survivable horizons, but it “does not directly steer the agent towards a positive reward” and may help only marginally in extremely sparse tasks [[208]](https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/26723/26495)(https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/26723/26495).

That distinction is crucial. Barrier and shield mechanisms are best seen as **feasibility enforcers** and **survivability enhancers**, not universal exploration solutions [[208]](https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/26723/26495)(https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/26723/26495) [[198]](https://tugraz.elsevierpure.com/ws/portalfiles/portal/93576031/Shields_for_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning.pdf)(https://tugraz.elsevierpure.com/ws/portalfiles/portal/93576031/Shields_for_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning.pdf). They improve the distribution of collected data mainly by preventing early termination and preserving recoverable states, not by discovering distant goals on their own.

## 4. Reachability and safe-set expansion: safety as feasible-region discovery

Another major trend is to represent safety not as average cost but as **reachable safe sets** or **largest feasible regions**. **RCRL** and **RESPO** define safety through persistent state constraints and feasible sets from which safety can be maintained indefinitely, explicitly criticizing expectation-based costs as too weak for applications where a single violation matters [[78]](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v162/yu22d/yu22d.pdf)(https://proceedings.mlr.press/v162/yu22d/yu22d.pdf) [[79]](https://neurips.cc/virtual/2023/poster/70926)(https://neurips.cc/virtual/2023/poster/70926) [[80]](https://papers.nips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/hash/dca63f2650fe9e88956c1b68440b8ee9-Abstract-Conference.html)(https://papers.nips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/hash/dca63f2650fe9e88956c1b68440b8ee9-Abstract-Conference.html). **VELM** similarly uses learned symbolic models plus reachability analysis and shielding to guarantee long-horizon safety over verified regions [[76]](https://herowanzhu.github.io/CAV_2024.pdf)(https://herowanzhu.github.io/CAV_2024.pdf) [[65]](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-65633-0_11)(https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-65633-0_11).

In continuous spaces, **ACTSAFE** and related GP-safe-set methods extend this idea to safe exploration under uncertainty. ACTSAFE assumes an initial safe seed set and then expands the safe policy set by learning the dynamics model while choosing, among currently safe policies, those that maximize epistemic information gain [[64]](https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2410.09486v2)(https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2410.09486v2). This is a rare example where intrinsic exploration is explicitly aligned with **safe-set expansion** rather than unrestricted novelty [[64]](https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2410.09486v2)(https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2410.09486v2).

The practical limitation is equally important: these methods usually need an initial safe set, calibrated uncertainty, and strong regularity assumptions, which are hard to satisfy in high-dimensional real robots without domain knowledge [[64]](https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2410.09486v2)(https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2410.09486v2) [[247]](https://las.inf.ethz.ch/files/turchetta16safemdp.pdf)(https://las.inf.ethz.ch/files/turchetta16safemdp.pdf) [[246]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2022/file/c628644624c1be9c8cfb1541fa6421fd-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2022/file/c628644624c1be9c8cfb1541fa6421fd-Paper-Conference.pdf). The planning interpretation is that safe-set expansion is powerful when safety can be localized geometrically or dynamically, but weak when safety depends on rich semantic context or high-dimensional perception.

## 5. Chance-constrained, distributionally robust, and risk-sensitive methods: when expected cost is the wrong safety object

When the safety requirement is probabilistic or tail-sensitive, expected cumulative costs are often semantically misaligned [[115]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf)(https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf) [[182]](https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~shangding.gu/tutorials/Safe_RL_2024_SmartGridComm.pdf)(https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~shangding.gu/tutorials/Safe_RL_2024_SmartGridComm.pdf) [[195]](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v168/pfrommer22a/pfrommer22a.pdf)(https://proceedings.mlr.press/v168/pfrommer22a/pfrommer22a.pdf). Chance-constrained methods bound the probability of entering unsafe states over a trajectory, which is more faithful to “crash probability” semantics [[115]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf)(https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/0913.pdf) [[195]](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v168/pfrommer22a/pfrommer22a.pdf)(https://proceedings.mlr.press/v168/pfrommer22a/pfrommer22a.pdf). Distributionally robust methods go further by protecting against misspecified disturbance distributions via ambiguity sets such as Wasserstein balls [[196]](https://zhengang-zhong.github.io/files/pdf/PSE.pdf)(https://zhengang-zhong.github.io/files/pdf/PSE.pdf) [[197]](https://ifatwww.et.uni-magdeburg.de/ifac2020/media/pdfs/4233.pdf)(https://ifatwww.et.uni-magdeburg.de/ifac2020/media/pdfs/4233.pdf). Risk-sensitive methods such as CVaR constrain or optimize the tail of the return or cost distribution rather than its mean [[86]](https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2022/0510.pdf)(https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2022/0510.pdf) [[87]](https://openreview.net/pdf/f44ab24699d9e5616dbd66b5cd5dda77e10c8ecd.pdf)(https://openreview.net/pdf/f44ab24699d9e5616dbd66b5cd5dda77e10c8ecd.pdf) [[88]](https://arxiv.org/html/2312.00342)(https://arxiv.org/html/2312.00342) [[265]](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v205/nishimura23a/nishimura23a.pdf)(https://proceedings.mlr.press/v205/nishimura23a/nishimura23a.pdf).

These formulations are especially relevant for planning under uncertainty, because they map directly onto risk-aware path planning and motion planning under uncertainty [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.17342v2) [[159]](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-981-99-6498-7_44)(https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-981-99-6498-7_44) [[265]](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v205/nishimura23a/nishimura23a.pdf)(https://proceedings.mlr.press/v205/nishimura23a/nishimura23a.pdf). Their main downside is complexity and conservatism: they are harder to optimize than standard CMDPs, often less scalable, and can easily over-restrict exploration if uncertainty sets or risk thresholds are tuned too pessimistically [[196]](https://zhengang-zhong.github.io/files/pdf/PSE.pdf)(https://zhengang-zhong.github.io/files/pdf/PSE.pdf) [[197]](https://ifatwww.et.uni-magdeburg.de/ifac2020/media/pdfs/4233.pdf)(https://ifatwww.et.uni-magdeburg.de/ifac2020/media/pdfs/4233.pdf) [[182]](https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~shangding.gu/tutorials/Safe_RL_2024_SmartGridComm.pdf)(https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~shangding.gu/tutorials/Safe_RL_2024_SmartGridComm.pdf).

The central design lesson is that **safety semantics must match deployment semantics**. If the real-world stakeholder cares about the worst 1% of episodes or the probability of any collision, reporting mean cost compliance is analytically weak even if the mean looks excellent.

## Where sparse rewards and constraints interact

The joint setting—**long-horizon sparse goals under explicit safety or feasibility limits**—is where the field is currently least unified and most practically important [[117]](https://tdsimao.github.io/assets/pdf/Yang2023reinforcement.pdf)(https://tdsimao.github.io/assets/pdf/Yang2023reinforcement.pdf) [[208]](https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/26723/26495)(https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/26723/26495) [[209]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/120ed726cf129dbeb8375b6f8a0686f8-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/120ed726cf129dbeb8375b6f8a0686f8-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[212]](https://neurips.cc/virtual/2024/101066)(https://neurips.cc/virtual/2024/101066) [[47]](https://openreview.net/pdf)(https://openreview.net/pdf). The most useful synthesis is to separate methods that **genuinely improve exploration** from methods that mainly **stabilize learning or preserve safety**.

The interaction is structurally antagonistic: sparse rewards demand broader state coverage, while safety constraints shrink the admissible set of trajectories. Any method that ignores this antagonism will overclaim. The strongest joint methods work precisely because they decide **where the agent is allowed to search** and **how it will search within that region**.

## 1. Methods that primarily stabilize learning or preserve feasibility

### Shielding under sparse rewards
As noted above, shielding improves sparse-reward learning mainly by **preventing early failure**, extending survivable episode length, and increasing the frequency with which sparse reward can even be encountered [[208]](https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/26723/26495)(https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/26723/26495). That is valuable, but it is not the same as learning better exploratory behavior [[208]](https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/26723/26495)(https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/26723/26495). Its main causal effect is survival-time extension, not state-coverage optimization.

### Safety-aware representations
Recent safe-representation papers argue that poor latent geometry can create false positives, over-pruning safe actions and making sparse-reward safe exploration unnecessarily conservative [[123]](https://openreview.net/pdf/6977411166b10cde440a2ea4b6d7cdd560e9de44.pdf)(https://openreview.net/pdf/6977411166b10cde440a2ea4b6d7cdd560e9de44.pdf) [[124]](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025arXiv250310318K/abstract)(https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025arXiv250310318K/abstract). These methods can recover exploration by better separating safe and unsafe regions in representation space, but a substantial part of their gain may come from **removing representational pathologies** rather than introducing deeper exploration principles [[123]](https://openreview.net/pdf/6977411166b10cde440a2ea4b6d7cdd560e9de44.pdf)(https://openreview.net/pdf/6977411166b10cde440a2ea4b6d7cdd560e9de44.pdf) [[124]](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025arXiv250310318K/abstract)(https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025arXiv250310318K/abstract).

### Barrier and CBF wrappers
In robot tasks with collision avoidance or connectivity constraints, learned or data-driven barrier functions preserve feasibility during learning and deployment [[75]](http://staff.ustc.edu.cn/~zkan/Papers/Conference/[42]_2022CAC.pdf)(http://staff.ustc.edu.cn/~zkan/Papers/Conference/ [[42]](https://realworld-sdm.github.io/paper/44.pdf)_2022CAC.pdf). Again, they are best interpreted as making exploration **admissible**, not as discovering long-horizon sparse solutions on their own [[75]](http://staff.ustc.edu.cn/~zkan/Papers/Conference/[42]_2022CAC.pdf)(http://staff.ustc.edu.cn/~zkan/Papers/Conference/ [[42]](https://realworld-sdm.github.io/paper/44.pdf)_2022CAC.pdf).

## 2. Methods that more directly expand exploratory coverage

### Safe guide transfer and behavior composition
**SaGui** is a strong example of a truly joint method. It first learns a task-agnostic safe guide in a reward-free constrained source task, then transfers that guide to a target sparse-reward task, distills it into a student policy, and composes guide and student behavior during learning [[117]](https://tdsimao.github.io/assets/pdf/Yang2023reinforcement.pdf)(https://tdsimao.github.io/assets/pdf/Yang2023reinforcement.pdf). This does more than stabilize policy updates: it changes the **state-action distribution** the learner experiences in the target task, making safe exploration of reward-relevant trajectories possible much earlier [[117]](https://tdsimao.github.io/assets/pdf/Yang2023reinforcement.pdf)(https://tdsimao.github.io/assets/pdf/Yang2023reinforcement.pdf).

That mechanism matters because many safe RL methods only censor actions. SaGui alters the *proposal distribution* itself. In practice, that is often what separates “safe but stuck” from “safe and progressing.”

### Curriculum over tasks or constraints
Curriculum methods such as **SCG** and **RECRL/CLiC** jointly address safety and sparse discoverability by modifying the training distribution [[209]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/120ed726cf129dbeb8375b6f8a0686f8-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/120ed726cf129dbeb8375b6f8a0686f8-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[210]](https://www.cs.utexas.edu/~pstone/Papers/bib2html-links/shahaf_shperberg_AAMAS_2024.pdf)(https://www.cs.utexas.edu/~pstone/Papers/bib2html-links/shahaf_shperberg_AAMAS_2024.pdf). **SCG** starts from low-cost contexts and only later emphasizes reward, aligning the curriculum with constrained objectives [[209]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/120ed726cf129dbeb8375b6f8a0686f8-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/120ed726cf129dbeb8375b6f8a0686f8-Paper-Conference.pdf). **CLiC** instead relaxes cost limits during training and then tightens them toward deployment thresholds, explicitly arguing that strict early constraints can trap learning in poor local optima [[210]](https://www.cs.utexas.edu/~pstone/Papers/bib2html-links/shahaf_shperberg_AAMAS_2024.pdf)(https://www.cs.utexas.edu/~pstone/Papers/bib2html-links/shahaf_shperberg_AAMAS_2024.pdf).

These are genuine exploration interventions because they alter which tasks are explored and how reachable the exploratory frontier is, rather than only changing the optimizer [[209]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/120ed726cf129dbeb8375b6f8a0686f8-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/120ed726cf129dbeb8375b6f8a0686f8-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[210]](https://www.cs.utexas.edu/~pstone/Papers/bib2html-links/shahaf_shperberg_AAMAS_2024.pdf)(https://www.cs.utexas.edu/~pstone/Papers/bib2html-links/shahaf_shperberg_AAMAS_2024.pdf).

### Constrained goal-conditioned planning
The NeurIPS 2024 multi-agent navigation method combines a **goal-conditioned safe policy** with a **graph planner** over replay-buffer states, pruning unsafe edges and using learned value functions for distance and safety [[212]](https://neurips.cc/virtual/2024/101066)(https://neurips.cc/virtual/2024/101066). This is a true long-horizon exploration method because it reduces horizon, structures route search, and handles safety at the planning layer rather than asking a flat policy to solve safe sparse navigation end-to-end [[212]](https://neurips.cc/virtual/2024/101066)(https://neurips.cc/virtual/2024/101066).

### Safe world models
Safe world-model work such as **CERL** uses Bayesian world models and pessimistic planning to keep learning within safe regions while using model-based lookahead for longer-horizon exploration [[47]](https://openreview.net/pdf)(https://openreview.net/pdf). This is promising because it combines planning depth with uncertainty sensitivity, but the paper itself cautions that strong assumptions are needed for formal guarantees and that state-wise safety remains harder than expected-cost safety in realistic settings [[47]](https://openreview.net/pdf)(https://openreview.net/pdf).

### Reset and collision-budget schemes
Recent navigation work shows that **reset assumptions** themselves can silently destroy exploration. Immediate reset on every collision prevents the learner from accumulating critical near-boundary or recovery experiences, especially in dense obstacle fields [[120]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.02192v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2605.02192v1) [[119]](https://arxiv.org/html/2603.09460v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2603.09460v1). Methods such as **multi-collision budgets** and **collision-state initialization** explicitly retain or replay hard states after local failure, thereby improving coverage of high-risk bottlenecks and accelerating learning [[120]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.02192v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2605.02192v1) [[119]](https://arxiv.org/html/2603.09460v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2603.09460v1). These are among the clearest cases where the exploration gain is structural and not merely optimizer stabilization [[120]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.02192v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2605.02192v1) [[119]](https://arxiv.org/html/2603.09460v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2603.09460v1).

A good practical litmus test is: **does the method change which states become reachable during training, or only how safely current states are visited?** If only the latter, it is usually a stabilizer, not a deep exploration method.

## Benchmark ecosystem, empirical evidence, and reproducibility

The benchmark picture is improving, but it is still fragmented.

## 1. Safe RL benchmarks are now more standardized than sparse-reward exploration benchmarks
