**Safety Gym** established a common online safe-exploration benchmark with 18 continuous-control environments plus 9 debugging environments, and recommended evaluating final task performance, final constraint satisfaction, and training-time safety regret [[6]](https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf)(https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf). **Safety-Gymnasium** extends this to 54 environments, vector and vision observations, single- and multi-agent settings, and pairs the environments with a 16-algorithm library (**SafePO**) for standardized comparisons [[216]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf) [[31]](https://openreview.net/forum)(https://openreview.net/forum).

For **offline safe RL**, the new FSRL/DSRL/OSRL benchmark provides 38 datasets across Safety-Gymnasium, BulletSafetyGym, and MetaDrive, and explicitly evaluates safety compliance, reward seeking, generalization, and outlier sensitivity [[217]](https://data.mlr.press/assets/pdf/v01-12.pdf)(https://data.mlr.press/assets/pdf/v01-12.pdf) [[218]](https://www.offline-saferl.org/)(https://www.offline-saferl.org/).

This is real progress: safe RL now has benchmark ecosystems that explicitly track **return under cost budgets**, **normalized cost**, **training-time safety**, and in some cases **generalization under data perturbation** [[6]](https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf)(https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf) [[216]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf) [[218]](https://www.offline-saferl.org/)(https://www.offline-saferl.org/). Equally important, these claims come from benchmark builders’ own dataset and environment papers rather than ad hoc blog-style comparisons, so the evidence quality is higher than in much older benchmark discussions.

## 2. Sparse-reward exploration remains benchmark-fragmented

Sparse-reward work still relies on a mosaic of suites: **MiniGrid**, **Procgen**, **MetaWorld**, **DeepMind Control**, **Atari hard-exploration games**, **Crafter/Craftax**, **Adroit**, **RoboMimic**, **AntMaze**, and more [[219]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/ad2b3b0963114adddbd2c97d6e74abb9-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/ad2b3b0963114adddbd2c97d6e74abb9-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[220]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2501.12627)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2501.12627) [[222]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/ecd92623ac899357312aaa8915853699-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/ecd92623ac899357312aaa8915853699-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[221]](https://openreview.net/pdf/6b4b98bf8a74ceab5aeeb4d8c40c0f864565bc6f.pdf)(https://openreview.net/pdf/6b4b98bf8a74ceab5aeeb4d8c40c0f864565bc6f.pdf) [[47]](https://openreview.net/pdf)(https://openreview.net/pdf). This is one reason cross-paper rankings are unstable: different benchmarks emphasize different bottlenecks—representation learning, long-horizon stitching, manipulation, memory, partial observability, or exact action-sequence discovery [[222]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/ecd92623ac899357312aaa8915853699-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/ecd92623ac899357312aaa8915853699-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[47]](https://openreview.net/pdf)(https://openreview.net/pdf) [[226]](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.10550v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2502.10550v2).

A major advance here is **OGBench**, which addresses offline goal-conditioned RL with 8 environment types, 85 datasets, and multi-capability evaluation over long-horizon reasoning, stitching, stochasticity, and high-dimensional inputs [[222]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/ecd92623ac899357312aaa8915853699-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/ecd92623ac899357312aaa8915853699-Paper-Conference.pdf). But there is still no universal sparse-reward exploration benchmark comparable in community role to what Safety Gym/Safety-Gymnasium became for safe RL [[222]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/ecd92623ac899357312aaa8915853699-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/ecd92623ac899357312aaa8915853699-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[6]](https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf)(https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf) [[216]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf).

The practical implication is that “state of the art” claims in sparse exploration are less transportable than safe-RL claims. An algorithm can lead on MiniGrid and underperform on MetaWorld or AntMaze because the relevant bottleneck changed from memory to control or from discrete bottlenecks to continuous stitching.

## 3. Credible performance patterns

Several empirical patterns appear repeatedly credible across sources.

### Structured exploration beats undirected novelty on long-horizon tasks
Methods that add **goal directionality, temporal structure, hierarchy, search memory, or planning correction** outperform naïve curiosity or entropy regularization on the hardest long-horizon sparse tasks [[26]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.19850v2) [[31]](https://openreview.net/forum)(https://openreview.net/forum) [[32]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/b111aa30ab71255946b19b6bd4e68939-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/b111aa30ab71255946b19b6bd4e68939-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[33]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/6d7c4a0727e089ed6cdd3151cbe8d8ba-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/6d7c4a0727e089ed6cdd3151cbe8d8ba-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[34]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/hash/31ceb5aed43e2ec1b132e389cc1dcb56-Abstract-Conference.html)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/hash/31ceb5aed43e2ec1b132e389cc1dcb56-Abstract-Conference.html) [[45]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.10995)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.10995). The most credible pattern is not a universal leaderboard, but a repeated failure of purely local novelty on tasks requiring remembered bottlenecks or task-relevant subgoal order.

### Safety layers improve survivability and sometimes convergence, but do not automatically solve sparse exploration
Shielding and barrier methods frequently reduce violations and can improve convergence by preventing crash-dominated data collection, yet they do not reliably solve deep sparse-reward problems without additional subgoal or planning structure [[208]](https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/26723/26495)(https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/26723/26495) [[198]](https://tugraz.elsevierpure.com/ws/portalfiles/portal/93576031/Shields_for_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning.pdf)(https://tugraz.elsevierpure.com/ws/portalfiles/portal/93576031/Shields_for_Safe_Reinforcement_Learning.pdf) [[75]](http://staff.ustc.edu.cn/~zkan/Papers/Conference/[42]_2022CAC.pdf)(http://staff.ustc.edu.cn/~zkan/Papers/Conference/ [[42]](https://realworld-sdm.github.io/paper/44.pdf)_2022CAC.pdf).

### Off-policy constrained RL can be sample-efficient, but only if cost estimation is handled conservatively
Recent off-policy safe RL papers repeatedly identify **cost underestimation** as the central failure mode and add uncertainty bounds, conservative critics, or adaptive multiplier logic to fix it [[192]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/2f8b56543953d60f262fb2c4b85c50b3-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/2f8b56543953d60f262fb2c4b85c50b3-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[94]](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v267/gao25b.html)(https://proceedings.mlr.press/v267/gao25b.html) [[101]](https://openreview.net/pdf/36111b7869598bcb726c1a06858a8fe3cf31cab5.pdf)(https://openreview.net/pdf/36111b7869598bcb726c1a06858a8fe3cf31cab5.pdf).

### Reward shaping heavily affects conclusions
Benchmarks such as Meta-World+, MTBench, and memory-intensive manipulation tasks show that reward density and design can dramatically change learning difficulty, gradient conflict, and apparent algorithm rankings [[109]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.11289v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2505.11289v2) [[225]](https://rlj.cs.umass.edu/2025/papers/RLJ_RLC_2025_140.pdf)(https://rlj.cs.umass.edu/2025/papers/RLJ_RLC_2025_140.pdf) [[226]](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.10550v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2502.10550v2). That means many reported gains are benchmark- and reward-definition-sensitive. A method that looks like a sparse-reward breakthrough on one shaped benchmark may mostly be exploiting shaping regularities.

### Real-robot evidence exists, but still favors hybridization
There are now real-robot results for sparse-reward transfer, safe quadrotor control, locomotion with RL-plus-safety overlays, and uncertainty-aware model-based RL on physical robots [[75]](http://staff.ustc.edu.cn/~zkan/Papers/Conference/[42]_2022CAC.pdf)(http://staff.ustc.edu.cn/~zkan/Papers/Conference/ [[42]](https://realworld-sdm.github.io/paper/44.pdf)_2022CAC.pdf) [[233]](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.04452v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2507.04452v1) [[274]](https://www.roboticsproceedings.org/rss18/p033.pdf)(https://www.roboticsproceedings.org/rss18/p033.pdf) [[273]](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.16680v3)(https://arxiv.org/html/2504.16680v3). But these successes are usually **modular, hybrid, or tightly engineered**, not pure end-to-end exploration systems [[272]](https://www.annualreviews.org/content/journals/10.1146/annurev-control-030323-022510)(https://www.annualreviews.org/content/journals/10.1146/annurev-control-030323-022510) [[274]](https://www.roboticsproceedings.org/rss18/p033.pdf)(https://www.roboticsproceedings.org/rss18/p033.pdf) [[273]](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.16680v3)(https://arxiv.org/html/2504.16680v3).

That last point is easy to understate. Real-robot success usually means the learning component solved a well-bounded subproblem—low-level locomotion, local avoidance, model error adaptation, or belief compression—inside a larger stack with resets, supervisors, or planners. That is a materially different claim from “end-to-end RL solved planning under sparse rewards and constraints.”

## 4. Reproducibility remains a live issue

Benchmark-focused work increasingly emphasizes line-by-line implementation checks, reference code, seeds, standardized thresholds, and released datasets/checkpoints [[216]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf)(https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/3c557a3d6a48cc99444f85e924c66753-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf) [[217]](https://data.mlr.press/assets/pdf/v01-12.pdf)(https://data.mlr.press/assets/pdf/v01-12.pdf) [[222]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/ecd92623ac899357312aaa8915853699-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/ecd92623ac899357312aaa8915853699-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[228]](https://cs.brown.edu/people/gdk/pubs/heclr_subgoaldiff.pdf)(https://cs.brown.edu/people/gdk/pubs/heclr_subgoaldiff.pdf). Yet older and even some newer benchmarks still rely on hand-tuned hyperparameters, task-specific rewards, or simulator-specific quirks that make replication and fair ranking difficult [[6]](https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf)(https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf) [[225]](https://rlj.cs.umass.edu/2025/papers/RLJ_RLC_2025_140.pdf)(https://rlj.cs.umass.edu/2025/papers/RLJ_RLC_2025_140.pdf) [[258]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.06940)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.06940) [[262]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.00577v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2407.00577v1). The field has improved within subcommunities, but not yet across sparse and safe RL jointly [[217]](https://data.mlr.press/assets/pdf/v01-12.pdf)(https://data.mlr.press/assets/pdf/v01-12.pdf) [[222]](https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/ecd92623ac899357312aaa8915853699-Paper-Conference.pdf)(https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2025/file/ecd92623ac899357312aaa8915853699-Paper-Conference.pdf) [[6]](https://cdn.openai.com/safexp-short.pdf)(https://cdn.openai.com
